body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

talking so this starts with a story that goes back almost 15 years I was doing remote screen sharing to my computer at home and at that time I had a slow DSL line it was only half a megabit and the performance was terrible which you'd kind of expect um but I measured the network around trip time and it was about 30 milliseconds but when I clicked on the menu it took two seconds for the screen to update so something was not quite right there and I assumed it was buffer blo because I just learned from Jim gtis about buffer blo but when I dug a bit deeper that wasn't it this was the problem the socket sand buffer on that machine defaulted to 128 kilobytes and uh that socket send buffer serves Dual Purpose it holds the data that's in flight but not yet acknowledged and that's important because you might have to retransmit it but it only needed about 3 kilobytes to serve that purpose the other 125 kilobytes were just dead space and the screen sharing demon would grab a frame compress it stick it in the buffer and the sockets interface would say please sir can I have some more and it would go frame stop it frame stop it and it ended up with two seconds backlog of frames that hadn't even left the ethernet port yet so once I understood that I talked to my colleagues at Apple we added a new socket option called the TCP not sent low Watermark and we kept the bandwidth delay product to hold the data was in flight because you need that to keep the pipe fully utilized but then we minimized the excess waiting on top of that and uh a couple of years later I did a demo at the Apple
